title: Shares
summary: Share valuation depends on expected dividends, the growth stage, and the return required by investors.

topics.title: Zero growth
topics.summary: Useful when the dividend expected for the next period stays stable over time.

sections.title: Stable model
sections.body: If the expected dividend does not change, the share behaves like a simple perpetuity. Today's price comes from dividing the next expected dividend by the required return.
bullets: Use it when no dividend growth is expected.
The relevant dividend is D1, the next expected dividend.
As k_s rises, the estimated price falls.

sections.title: What each variable means
sections.body: Before calculating, clearly separate what each symbol represents in the model.

exampleTable.title: Model variables

cells: It is the present value you want to estimate.
Not the historical dividend, but the next expected cash flow.
Return investors demand for taking the share's risk.

exampleTable.footnote: This model is useful when dividend policy is very stable and no growth is expected.

sections.title: When to use it and what to avoid
sections.body: It is a simple model, but it only works well when its assumptions hold.
bullets: Use it when the company pays regular dividends with no expected growth.
Do not confuse D₁ with the last dividend paid if the problem asks for the next dividend.
If the business is growing, move to a growth model instead.

topics.title: Constant growth
topics.summary: Useful when the company can sustain a growth rate lower than the required return.

sections.title: Gordon model
sections.body: When the company grows at a constant rate g, today's price comes from discounting a growing perpetuity. The key condition is that g must remain below k_s.
bullets: D₁ represents the next expected dividend.
The difference k_s - g concentrates the relationship between risk and growth.
If g gets too close to k_s, price becomes very sensitive.

sections.title: Variables and financial reading
sections.body: The model does not only produce a price. It also shows how expected growth can lift the value of the share.

exampleTable.title: Variable reading

cells: Higher expected dividend means higher price.
Higher required return means lower price.
Higher sustainable growth means higher price.

exampleTable.footnote: The model loses validity if g is equal to or greater than k_s.

sections.title: Mini guided case
sections.body: If D₁ = 3, k_s = 15%, and g = 5%, the denominator becomes 10% and the estimated price is 30.

exampleTable.title: Case reading

cells: Next expected cash flow for shareholders.
Return required for the share's risk.
Current estimated value under constant growth.

exampleTable.footnote: The key lesson is not memorizing 30, but understanding that the spread between k_s and g drives value.

topics.title: Variable growth
topics.summary: Used when the company moves from a high-growth phase to a more stable terminal rate.

sections.title: Calculation logic by stages
sections.body: In this model a single formula is not enough. First project dividends during the initial phase and then compute a terminal value using a more stable rate.
bullets: The dividend input is D₀, the last dividend already paid.
g₁ can be a high rate during a short initial phase.
The terminal rate g₂ must be lower than k_s for the terminal value to make sense.

sections.title: How to solve it step by step
sections.body: This is the most useful model for companies in transition, but it also requires the most structure.
bullets: Project dividends from t = 1 through t = N using g₁.
Discount each of those dividends to the present using k_s.
Compute Dₙ₊₁ with the terminal growth rate g₂ and then estimate Pₙ.
Discount Pₙ to the present and add it to the discounted dividends.

sections.title: Model variables by stage
sections.body: Here it is not enough to identify one dividend and one rate. You must separate the initial phase from the terminal phase.

exampleTable.title: Key variables

cells: It is the base used to project D₁, D₂, and so on.
It can be higher than the terminal rate and last only a few periods.
Marks the end of the first stage.
It must be lower than k_s for the terminal value to be valid.
Used to discount both dividends and terminal value.

exampleTable.footnote: This model teaches that not every company grows at the same rate forever.

topics.title: Preferred shares
topics.summary: If the preferred dividend is fixed, valuation resembles a simple perpetuity.

sections.title: Preferred dividends
sections.body: Preferred shares usually pay a fixed dividend. If that cash flow is stable, valuation follows a perpetuity structure.
bullets: The relevant dividend here is D, not D₀ or D₁.
The required return reflects the risk of the preferred instrument.

sections.title: What to watch in a preferred share
sections.body: Even though the formula is simple, you still need to separate the fixed cash flow from the rate demanded by the market.

exampleTable.title: Model variables

cells: Value of the preferred share today.
Periodic cash flow promised to investors.
Return that compensates for the instrument's risk.

exampleTable.footnote: Conceptually it resembles the zero-growth model, but here the fixed dividend is treated as a perpetual preferred payment.

calculator.title: Share calculator
calculator.summary: Choose the right model and use the dividend definition, growth, and required return that belong to that case.
calculator.note: Each mode uses a different dividend input: D₁ for zero and constant growth, D₀ for variable growth, and D for preferred shares.

modes.label: Zero growth
modes.summary: Simple perpetuity with fixed D₁ and no growth.

modes.label: Constant growth
modes.summary: Gordon model with D₁ and a growth rate g lower than k_s.

modes.label: Variable growth
modes.summary: Two stages: an initial phase with g₁ and a terminal phase with g₂.

modes.label: Preferred
modes.summary: Perpetuity with fixed dividend D and no growth.

fields.label: Expected dividend next year (D₁)
fields.helperText: Use the next expected dividend, not the historical one.

fields.label: Required return (k_s, %)
fields.helperText: Return investors demand for the share's risk.

fields.label: Expected dividend next year (D₁)
fields.helperText: This model starts from the next expected dividend.

fields.label: Required return (k_s, %)
fields.helperText: It must stay above the constant growth rate.

fields.label: Constant growth rate (g, %)
fields.helperText: Use a sustainable rate that remains below k_s.

fields.label: Last dividend paid (D₀)
fields.helperText: This model starts from the last dividend already paid.

fields.label: Required return (k_s, %)
fields.helperText: It is used to discount projected dividends and terminal value.

fields.label: Initial growth (g₁, %)
fields.helperText: Rate used only during the first stage.

fields.label: Number of periods (N)
fields.helperText: Number of years in the first stage.

fields.label: Terminal growth (g₂, %)
fields.helperText: It must be lower than k_s for the terminal value to stay valid.

fields.label: Annual preferred dividend (D)
fields.helperText: Fixed cash flow paid by the preferred share.

fields.label: Required return (k_s, %)
fields.helperText: Return demanded for the preferred security's risk.
